BURDEN OF PROOF: Golden thread which permeates through web of criminal jurisprudence is that the accused is presumed to be innocent unless proved guilty. The prosecution must bring cogent and credible evidence to prove the charges 'beyond reasonable doubt'. There is a long mental distance between "may be true" and "must be true". This basic and golden rule only helps to maintain the vital distinction between "conjectures" and "conclusions". The prosecution is required to prove its case beyond 'reasonable doubt' and not 'all doubts'. The evidence must be 'weighed' and not 'counted'. FIR No. State vs. Fahim @ Bhoma & Anr. Page No. 9 of 22 Indian Kanoon - 6 In Ashish Batham vs. State of M.P., (2002) 7 SCC 317, Hon'ble Supreme Court of India held, as under: "8. Realities or truth apart, the fundamental and basic presumption in the administration of criminal law and justice delivery system is the innocence of the alleged accused and till the charges are proved beyond reasonable doubt on the basis of clear, cogent, credible or unimpeachable evidence, the question of indicating or punishing an accused does not arise, merely carried away by the heinous nature of the crime or the gruesome manner in which it was found to have been committed. Mere suspicion, however strong or probable it may be is no effective substitute for the legal proof required to substantiate the charge of commission of a crime and graver the charge is, greater should be the standard of proof required. Courts dealing with criminal cases at least should constantly remember that there is a long mental distance between "may be true" and "must be true" and this basic and golden rule only helps to maintain the vital distinction between "conjectures" and "sure conclusions" to be arrived at on the touchstone of a dispassionate judicial scrutiny based upon a complete and comprehensive appreciation of all features of the case as well as quality and credibility of the evidence brought on record." In Sheila Sebastian vs. R. Jawaharaj and Another, (2018) 7 SCC 581, Hon'ble Supreme Court of India held, as under: "27. A reasonable doubt has already been thoroughly explained in Latesh v. State of Maharashtra wherein "reasonable doubt" has been enunciated by this Court as (at SCC p. 83, para 46) "a mean between excessive caution and excessive indifference to a doubt, further it has been elaborated that reasonable doubt must be a practical one and not an abstract theoretical hypothesis." In Collector of Customs, Madras & Ors. vs. D. Bhoormall, (1974) 2 SCC 54, Hon'ble Supreme Court of India held, as under: "30.....the prosecution or the Department is not required to prove its case with mathematical precision to a demonstrable degree; for, in all human affairs absolute certainty is a myth, and - as Prof. Brett felicitously puts it - "all exactness is a fake". FIR No. State vs. Fahim @ Bhoma & Anr. Page No. 10 of 22 El Dorado of absolute proof being unattainable, the law accepts for it probability as a working substitute in this work-a-day world. The law does not require the prosecution to prove the impossible. All that it requires is the establishment of such a degree of probability that a prudent man may, on its basis, believe in the existence of the fact in issue. Thus, legal proof is not necessarily perfect proof; often it is nothing more than a prudent man's estimate as to the probabilities of the case. Indian Kanoon - 7 The other cardinal principle having an important bearing on the incidence of burden of proof is that sufficiency and weight of the evidence is to be considered - to use the words of Lord Mansfield in Blatch v. Archer - "according to the proof which it was in the power of one side to prove, and in the power of the other to have contradicted"....." In Latesh alias Dadu Baburao Karlekar v. State of Maharashtra, (2018) 3 SCC 66, Hon'ble Supreme Court of India held, as under: "46. In our opinion, an ingenious mind can question anything and, on the other hand, there is nothing which it cannot convince. When you consider the facts, you have a reasonable doubt as to whether the matter is proved or whether it is not a reasonable doubt in this sense. The reasonableness of a doubt must be a practical one and not on an abstract theoretical hypothesis. Reasonableness is a virtue that forms as a mean between excessive caution and excessive indifference to a doubt." APPRECIATION OF EVIDENCE: In Munna Lal vs. State of Uttar Pradesh, 2023 SCC OnLine SC 80, Hon'ble Supreme Court of India delineated principles for appreciation of evidence, as under: "28. Before embarking on the exercise of deciding the fate of these appellants, it would be apt to take note of certain principles relevant for a decision on these two appeals. Needless to observe, such principles have evolved over the years and crystallized into 'settled principles of law'. These are: (a). Section 134 of Indian Evidence Act, 1872, enshrines the well-recognized maxim that evidence has to be weighed and not counted. In other words, it is the quality of evidence that matters and not the quantity. FIR No. State vs. Fahim @ Bhoma & Anr. Page No. 11 of 22 As a sequitur, even in a case of murder, it is not necessary to insist upon a plurality of witnesses and the oral evidence of a single witness, if found to be reliable and trustworthy, could lead to a conviction. (b). Generally speaking, oral testimony may be classified into three categories, viz.: (i) Wholly reliable; (ii) Wholly unreliable; (iii) Neither wholly reliable nor wholly unreliable. The first two category of cases may not pose serious difficulty for the court in arriving at its conclusion(s). However, in the third category of cases, the court has to be circumspect and look for corroboration of any material particulars by reliable testimony, direct or circumstantial, as a Indian Kanoon - 8 requirement of the rule of prudence. (c). A defective investigation is not always fatal to the prosecution where ocular testimony is found credible and cogent. While in such a case the court has to be circumspect in evaluating the evidence, a faulty investigation cannot in all cases be a determinative factor to throw out a credible prosecution version. (d). Non-examination of the Investigating Officer must result in prejudice to the accused; if no prejudice is caused, mere non-examination would not render the prosecution case fatal. (e). Discrepancies do creep in, when a witness deposes in a natural manner after lapse of some time, and if such discrepancies are comparatively of a minor nature and do not go to the root of the prosecution story, then the same may not be given undue importance." EVIDENTIARY VALUE OF INJURED WITNESSES: Testimony of an injured witness has been accorded a special status in law. The fact that PW-2 HC Satpal received injuries establishes his presence at the scene of crime. In other words, presence of injury is an inbuilt guarantee of his presence at the time of incident. His evidence cannot be discarded in absence of compelling reasons. He would not spare actual assailant in order to falsely implicate someone. FIR No. State vs. Fahim @ Bhoma & Anr. Page No. 12 of 22 In Sadakat Kotwar & Anr. vs. State of Jharkhand, 2021 SCC OnLine SC 1046, Hon'ble Supreme Court of India held, as under: "2.....As held by this Court in the case of State of M.P. vs. Mansingh, (2003) 10 SCC 414 para 9, the evidence of an injured eye-witness has great evidentiary value and unless compelling reasons exist, their statements are not to be discarded lightly....." In Lakshman Singh vs. State of Bihar, (2021) 9 SCC 191, Hon'ble Supreme Court of India held, as under: "9.1.....It is further observed that "where witness to occurrence was himself injured in the incident, testimony of such witness is generally considered to be very reliable, as he is a witness that comes with an inbuilt guarantee of his presence at the scene of crime and is unlikely to spare his actual assailant(s) in order to falsely implicate someone". It is further observed that "thus, deposition of injured witness should be relied upon unless there are strong grounds for rejection of his evidence on basis of major contradictions and discrepancies therein. 9.2. The aforesaid principle of law has been reiterated again by this Court in Ramvilas and it is held that "evidence of injured witnesses is entitled to a great weight and very cogent and convincing grounds are required to discard their evidence". It is further observed that "being Indian Kanoon - 9 injured witnesses, their presence at the time and place of occurrence cannot be doubted". In Baleshwar Mahto and Another vs. State of Bihar and Another, (2017) 3 SCC 152, Hon'ble Supreme Court of India held, as under: "12.....When the eyewitness is also an injured person, due credence to his version needs to be accorded. On this aspect, we may refer to the following observations in Abdul Sayeed v. State of M.P. : (SCC pp. 271-72, paras 28-30)" The evidence of an injured witness must be given due weightage. His statement is generally considered to be very reliable. It is unlikely that he would spare the actual assailants in order to falsely implicate someone else. FIR No. State vs. Fahim @ Bhoma & Anr. LEGAL PROVISION: Statutory provision applicable to this case is, as under: "307. Attempt to murder. - Whoever does any act with such intention or knowledge, and under such circumstances that, if he by that act caused death, he would be guilty of murder, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term Indian Kanoon - 12 which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine; and if hurt is caused to any person by such act, the offender shall be liable either to [imprisonment for life], or to such punishment as is hereinbefore mentioned." FIR No. State vs. Fahim @ Bhoma & Anr. Page No. 17 of 22 JUDICIAL PRECEDENT: In Surender Singh vs. State (Union Territory of Chandigarh), 2021 SCC OnLine SC 1135, Hon'ble Supreme Court of India held, as under: "18.....In State of Madhya Pradesh v. Saleem @ Chamaru, this Court, while re-appreciating the true import of Section 307 held as follows: "12. To justify a conviction under this section, it is not essential that bodily injury capable of causing death should have been inflicted. Although the nature of injury actually caused may often give considerable assistance in coming to a finding as to the intention of the accused, such intention may also be deduced from other circumstances, and may even, in some cases, be ascertained without any reference at all to actual wounds. The section makes a distinction between an act of the accused and its result, if any. Such an act may not be attended by any result so far as the person assaulted is concerned, but still there may be cases in which the culprit would be liable under this section. It is not necessary that the injury actually caused to the victim of the assault should be sufficient under ordinary circumstances to cause the death of the person assaulted. What the court has to see is whether the act, irrespective of its result, was done with the intention or knowledge and under circumstances mentioned in the section. An attempt in order to be criminal need not be the penultimate act. It is sufficient in law, if there is present an intent coupled with some overt act in execution thereof. It is sufficient to justify a conviction under Section 307 if there is present an intent coupled with some overt act in execution thereof. It is not essential that bodily injury capable of causing death should have been inflicted. The section makes a distinction between the act of the accused and its result, if any. The court has to see whether the act, irrespective of its result, was done with the intention or knowledge and under circumstances mentioned in the section. Therefore, an accused charged under Section 307 IPC cannot be acquitted merely because the injuries inflicted on the victim were in the nature of a simple hurt." FIR No. State vs. Fahim @ Bhoma & Anr. Page No. 18 of 22 In Suryakant Baburao @ Ramrao vs. State of Maharashtra and Others, (2020) 17 SCC 518, Hon'ble Supreme Court of India held, as under: "8.....To justify conviction under Section 307 IPC, intention of causing death or that it was done with the intention of causing such injury which is likely to cause death is Indian Kanoon - 13 necessary to constitute the offence. Although the nature of injury actually caused would be of considerable assistance in coming to a finding as to the intention of the accused. such intention may also be deduced from other circumstances." It is by now a lucid dictum that for the purpose of constituting an offence under Section 307 IPC, there are two ingredients that a Court must consider, first, whether there was any intention or knowledge on the part of the accused to cause death of the victim, and, second, such intent or knowledge was followed by some overt actus rea in execution thereof, irrespective of the consequential result as to whether or not any injury is inflicted upon the victim. The Courts may deduce such intent from the conduct of the accused and surrounding circumstances of offence, including the nature of weapon used or the nature of injury, if any. The manner in which occurrence took place may enlighten more than the prudential escape of a victim. It is thus not necessary that a victim shall have to suffer an injury dangerous to his life, for attracting Section 307 IPC. INGREDIENTS OF SECTION 307 IPC: In order to attract Section 307 IPC, the prosecution has to establish: (i) the intention or knowledge to commit murder; and (ii) the act done by the accused. FIR No. State vs. Fahim @ Bhoma & Anr. Counsel for accused and perused the record. ANALYSIS OF EVIDENCE AND FINDINGS The accused is facing trial for the offence U/s. IPC and 27 of the Arms Act. For the sake of convenience, the relevant section is reproduced below:- Section 307 IPC deals with attempt to murder. It states that "Whoever does any act with such intention or knowledge, and under such circumstances that, if he by that act caused death, he would be guilty of murder, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine; and if hurt is caused to any person by such act, the offender shall be liable either to imprisonment for life, or to such punishment as is hereinbefore mentioned". 1. The essential ingredients required to be proved in the case of an offence u/s. 307 IPC are:- (i) that the death of a human being was attempted; (ii) that such death was attempted to be caused by or in SC NO. FIR NO. , PS. JAMIA NAGAR STATE VS. MOHD. SHOAIB GEETANJALI Date: consequence of the act of the accused; (iii) that such act was done with the intention of causing death or that it was done with the intention of causing such bodily injury as (a) the accused knew to be likely to cause death; or (b) was sufficient in the ordinary course of nature to cause death or that the accused attempted to cause death by doing an act known to him to be so imminently dangerous that it must in all probability cause (a) death or (b) such bodily injury as is likely to cause death, the accused having no excuse for incurring the risk of causing such death or injury. 2. Section 27 of Arms Act deals with punishment for using arms, etc. (1) Whoever uses any arms or ammunition in contravention of section 5 shall be punishable with imprisonment for a term which shall not be less than three years but which may extend to seven years and shall also be liable to fine. Indian Kanoon - 7 (2) Whoever uses any prohibited arms or prohibited ammunition in contravention of section 7 shall be punishable with imprisonment for a term which shall not be less than seven years but which may extend to imprisonment for life and shall also be liable to fine. (3) Whoever uses any prohibited arms or prohibited ammunition or does any act in contravention of section 7 and such use or act results in the death of any other person, [shall be punishable with imprisonment for life, GEETANJALI Date: SC NO. FIR NO. , PS. JAMIA NAGAR STATE VS. MOHD. SHOAIB or death and shall also be liable to fine.]. 3. It is well settled law that while appreciating the evidence of witness, the approach must be whether the evidence of a witness read as a whole appears to have a ring of truth. Once that impression is found, it is undoubtedly necessary for the Court to scrutinize the evidence more particularly keeping in view the deficiencies, draw backs and infirmities pointed out in the evidence as a whole and evaluate them to find out whether it is against the general tenor of the evidence. Minor discrepancies on trivial matters not touching the core of the case, hyper technical approach by taking sentences torn out of context here or there from the evidence, attaching importance to some technical error committed by the investigating officer not going to the root of the matter should not ordinarily permit rejection of the evidence as a whole (Reliance placed on the judgment of Hon'ble Supreme Court of India in State of UP vs Krishna Master & Ors, decided on /2010 in Cr Appeal No. ). The case of the prosecution is that accused Mohd. Shoaib alongwith two other associates namely Guddu and Bilal (not charge- sheeted) in furtherance of their common intention abused, wrongfully restrained and fired from the country-made pistol towards the complainant Arshad thereby causing simple injury on his right arm with such intention and under such circumstance if death of Arshad is caused, accused Mohd. Shoaib may be guilty of murder. In order to prove its case the prosecution has examined injured/ complainant Sh. Arshad and other public witnesses Sh. Mohsin and Ms. Mehtab. GEETANJALI Date: SC NO. It has been further argued that that the alleged weapon i.e. gun allegedly used by the accused, has not been recovered by the IO so far. However it is well settled principle of law that non recovery of the weapon of offence has no effect on the merit of the case. Mere non-recovery of weapons does not adversely affect the credibility of the prosecution story. It is now well settled that the recovery of weapon is not essential for establishing that the weapon was used in commission of the crime. In Rajender and Others Vs. State of Haryana & Anr. (2005) 9 SCC 784, the weapon of offence was not recovered by the police from the possession of any of the appellants. Hon'ble Supreme Court however, held that this could not be a ground to throw out the prosecution case, when the same has been otherwise found to be truthful by credible evidence. 10.12. It has been further argued that as per the prosecution story it has been alleged that "the complainant received a phone call from mobile no.7210925798 on his mobile no.9540224629 made by accused Shoaib and he asked him to come to meet him near Al taqwa Digitally signed by GEETANJALI GEETANJALI Date: 09.23 SC NO. FIR NO. , PS. JAMIA NAGAR STATE VS. MOHD. SHOAIB 16:28:22 +0530 masjid, Jogabai; that the CDR of the aforesaid mobile phone has not been placed on record by the IO deliberately as no such alleged calls has ever been made by the accused to the complainant on the alleged date and time; that PW-10 SI Varun has stated in his cross- examination that it is correct that he obtained the CDR/ location of the mobile number provided by the injured Arshad in his complaint which he stated belongs to the accused Mohd. Shoaib ; that the location of the mobile number was not at the place of occurrence. It has been well proved by the ocular evidence that accused Mohd. Shoaib attacked complainant Sh. Arshad Parvez with gun and CDR has only supporting or corroborative evidentiary value. Secondly, accused did not concrete his defence by producing the said CDRs during trial. Indian Kanoon - 12 13. It has been further argued that the alleged place i.e. Al taqwa masjid, Jogabai extension is densely populated area and several shops were opened at that time as alleged by the Prosecution however the Prosecution has not examined any independent public witnesses from the neighborhood of the incident. The present FIR was registered on the complaint of Sh. Arshad who was examined as PW-6 and he has very well explained what happened on the date of alleged incident. Not only that he withstood the rigours of cross examination and accused failed to shake his credibility during cross examination. The testimony of the complainant cannot be discarded merely on the ground that no public persons were joined in the investigation. Secondly, it is a common that generally public is averse to become a witness in a criminal case because of the attitude of the CourtsDigitally in signed by GEETANJALI GEETANJALI Date: 09.23 SC NO. FIR NO. , PS. JAMIA NAGAR STATE VS. MOHD. SHOAIB 16:28:28 +0530 summoning the witnesses time and again and sending them back on the ground that either the counsel for the accused was not available or accused was not there. Hence the case of the prosecution cannot be rejected on the ground of non examination of public witness or on account of non joining of public witness. (Reliance placed on judgment titled as Appabhai & Anr. Vs. State of Gujarat, IR 1988 SC 696). Moving forward to trace out any link, if any, of the circumstantial evidence I shall delve upon the other vital piece of evidence. MEDICAL EVIDENCE The fact that PW-6 suffered injuries on his right arm has been proved by PW-8 Sh. Rajender Singh, Medical Record Clerk, AIIMS through MLC Ex. PW8/1. The MLC described the injuries as wound 1- others entry wound, lateral accept of RT Arm, 2X1 CM and wound 2- swelling exit wound, lateral accept of RT Arm, 0.5X0.5 cm. 12.1. The opinion on the MLC was proved by PW-4 Dr. Krishna Kumar Singh, Sr. Resident, AIIMS. PW-4 has deposed that "on 16.11.2018 he received a request for subsequent opinion from ASI Bhagwan Singh, PS Jamia Nagar along with the MLC of patient Arshad, photocopy of discharge summary, X-ray report and radiology report; that after perusal of above said available documents he was of the considered opinion that the injury was simple in nature and could be possible by gun shot; that he had prepared the subsequent opinion dated 16.11.2018 which is Ex. PW4/A". Hence he has proved the nature of injury as simple and could be possibly by gun shot. Digitally signed by GEETANJALI GEETANJALI Date: Indian Kanoon - 13 09.23 SC NO. FIR NO. , PS. JAMIA NAGAR STATE VS. MOHD. SHOAIB 16:28:34 +0530 Secondly, he has described both the entry and exit wound as per the photograph in the MLC which is only in the gun shot injury. Thirdly, he has denied the suggestion that the injury mentioned in the MLC may be caused by putting iron rod or any sharp weapon. 12.2. During the course of arguments, it was submitted by Ld. Defence Counsel that the name of the accused has nowhere mentioned in the MLC nor he disclosed the name of the assailant to the doctor. Further, the religion of the injured is wrongly mentioned as Hindu who is a Muslim i.e. injured Arshad or that the person who brought the injured to the hospital has not been cited as witness by the Prosecution. Firstly, this is not the bounden duty of doctor to describe the history in detail whose work is only to save the life. The non- mentioning of the name of the accused in the MLC is not giving any benefit to the accused. Secondly, the testimony of the person who brought the injured to the hospital is not at all relevant in deciding the guilt of the accused. It has further argued that no photograph of the entry and exit wound of injured were taken nor were placed on record. This aspect has been clarified by PW-9 Dr. Aswin who has deposed that "at the time of examination injured Mr. Arshad was having entry wound over lateral aspect of right arm 2X1 cm and exit wound over lateral aspect of right arm 0.5X0.5 cm. and the injury was simple in nature opined to be caused by fire arm ". Further during cross- examination he has admitted that entry exit wounds are not clear in the photographs which possibly lead to the conclusion that both the wounds are shown in one photograph. Henceforth it has been well proved that complainant i.e. PW-6 sustained gun shot injury. Digitally signed by GEETANJALI GEETANJALI Date: 09.23 16:28:41 +0530 SC NO. FIR NO. , PS. JAMIA NAGAR STATE VS. MOHD. SHOAIB 12.3. It has been further argued by Ld. Defence Counsel that no gun shot residue was found on the swab taken from the alleged and entry wound. Accused has admitted the FSL result dated 29.06.2020 which is Ex. A-4. Perusal of the FSL report reveals that the clothes of the acussed i.e. full sleeves shirt, sandow baniyan and lower were also sent for examination alongwith swab taken from the right hand of the injured Arshad. The clothes of the injured were described as Ex. 1. The FSL report concluded that: "on the basis of physical and chemical examination conducted in the laboratory, it is opined that gun shot residue (lead) could be detected from the hole (marked H-1) observed on the right arm of the shirt contained in Ex. No.1". 4. The fact that gun shot residue was detected on the shirt of the injured and not on the swab can very well be justified by the kind of the injury sustained by Arshad i.e. PW-6 which is simple injury. DEFENCE OF THE ACCUSED Indian Kanoon - 14 In his statement u/s. 313 Cr.PC, the accused has stated that it is a false case; that he has been falsely implicated in the present case; that he was doing construction business with partnership with Sh. Arshad; that he was demanding five to six lacs and when he denied after sometime he implicated him in the present false case. However he preferred not to lead any evidence. Secondly, accused has nowhere described what was the reason behind the false implication or what was the cause of enmity between them. 13.1. In order to prove the offence under section 307 IPC it Digitally is signed by GEETANJALI GEETANJALI Date: 09.23 SC NO. FIR NO. , PS. JAMIA NAGAR STATE VS. MOHD. SHOAIB 16:28:48 +0530 essential to prove that there was intention or knowledge coupled with some overt act in execution of such intention / knowledge. It has been held by the Hon'ble Supreme Court in the matter of State of Madhya Pradesh v. Kedar Yadav, 2009 (17) SCC 280, that "for conviction under section 307 IPC it is sufficient if there is an intention or knowledge coupled with some overt act in execution of such intention/ knowledge. It is not necessary that bodily injury capable of causing death should have been inflicted. If the act, irrespective of its result is done with the intention or knowledge and under the circumstances mentioned in the sections, the same may result conviction under section 307 IPC and the accused cannot be acquitted merely because the injuries inflicted on the victim were in the nature of a simple hurt". 2. In the matter of Satish Kumar v. State (NCT of Delhi), 2013 (5) AD (Delhi) 674 : 2013 (3) JCC 1749, the Hon'ble Delhi High Court observed as under:- "8. The issue now arises is whether an offence under Section 307 IPC is made out or not. The essential ingredient of Section 307 IPC is the mensrea. Mensrea can be inferred from the kind of weapon used, the place of injury, motive etc. To bring home a charge under Section 307 IPC the onus lies on the prosecution to prove that the accused caused an act with the intention or knowledge and under such circumstances that if by that act death was caused he would be guilty of murder. In the present case on a quarrel ensuing, the Appellant caused a knife blow on the injured by a kitchen knife. The injury was caused on the back side and only one blow was given. In view of the weapon of offence and injury not being on the vital part it cannot be said that the Appellant had intention to cause murder of the injured. Further the Appellant certainly had intention to cause injury GEETANJALI Date: SC NO. FIR NO. , PS. JAMIA NAGAR STATE VS. MOHD. SHOAIB +0530 which was opined to be simple sharp in nature. Thus the Appellant is convicted for offence punishable under Section 324 IPC instead of Section 307 IPC and the conviction under Section 307 IPC is set aside". 3. Viewed in this light, the testimonies of the witnesses are now to be examined to consider whether the conviction can be sustained under section 307 IPC on the basis of the evidence led in the present matter. All the ingredients of Section 307 IPC ought to be fulfilled before the conviction Indian Kanoon - 15 can be sustained under section 307 IPC. The injuries sustained, the manner of assault and the weapon used are all relevant factors. The foremost ingredients of section 307 IPC is the intention or knowledge which must precede the act attributed to the accused. In order to determine the intention of the assailant various factors and circumstances are to be considered such as the nature of injury caused, severity of the blow or its persistence, sufficiency of time and opportunity of causing more injury, intervention by others, nature of weapon and the seat of injuries. The intention / knowledge has to be gathered from the surroundings circumstances and not necessarily from the results that follow, which may result in simple or grievous injury. The onus lies on the prosecution that the accused has caused an act with intention/knowledge and under such circumstances that if by that act death was caused, he would be guilty of murder. 13.4. In the given facts and circumstances the testimony of the complainant is well corroborated by the testimonies of the other witnesses and the fact that accused assaulted the complainant has been well established. However neither it has been proved that the assault was premeditated or the assault was made with intention to commit Digitally signed by GEETANJALI GEETANJALI Date: 09.23 SC NO. FIR NO. , PS. JAMIA NAGAR STATE VS. MOHD. SHOAIB 16:29:11 PAGE NO. 26 OF+0530 culpable homicide not amounting to murder. Secondly, PW-8 Sh. Rajender Singh, Medical Record Clerk, AIIMS has proved the MLC which is Ex. PW8/1. The MLC describes injuries as wound 1- others entry wound, lateral accept of RT Arm, 2X1 CM and wound 2- swelling exit wound, lateral accept of RT Arm, 0.5X0.5 cm . From the nature injuries it cannot be said that they were caused with avowed object or knowledge to cause death. As per MLC report of the injured/complainant there is no opinion that the injuries are such that same are likely to cause the death of the injured rather they were stated to be simple. Hence the ingredients of section 307 IPC are not fulfilled and the case falls within the ambit of section 324 IPC which deals with voluntarily causing hurt by dangerous weapons or means which is the gun in the present case. In view of the aforesaid discussion, it is hereby held that Prosecution has failed to bring home the guilt of the accused for the offence punishable under section IPC and the case falls under section IPC. Henceforth accused Mohd. Shoaib is acquitted of offence punishable u/s. IPC and convicted under section IPC and Section 27 of Arms Act. Sessions Case No. Page ASJ-04/East/KKD/Delhi 14) Accused Sumit @ Chatela further stated that the complainant used to create commotion and abuse him from outside his house after consuming liquor. Accused used to object to his behaviour and as such the complainant used to keep enmity towards him. Accused still had a cut mark on his face. He never caused any injury to the complainant and rather he himself received injury but he was falsely implicated in the present case. 15) I have heard the arguments of ld. Counsel for the accused persons and Ld. Addl.P.P. for the State and perused the record. Testimonies of material witnesses : 16) Injured Ravi was examined as PW2. He deposed that he was residing at , Trilokpuri, Delhi for the last 14-15 years and was working as a Driver. He deposed that on 02.07.2016, he had gone to take a cold drink from sewing centre, near park after having his meal. At about 10:15 p.m, accused persons namely Rahish, Sumit @ Chatela and Shiva (since deceased), whom he correctly identified before the court, being resident of his locality, they alongwith one Chikna and another person, whose name he did not know, but could identify him if shown to him, all came there. They all had knives in their hands. They all rebuked him in filthy language and when PW2 asked them as to why they were rebuking him, then all the accused persons alongwith their Sessions Case No. Page ASJ-04/East/KKD/Delhi associates surrounded (gheraoed) him and accused Shiva threatened by saying that "saale aaj tujhe jaan se markar ilaqe me dahshat paida karenge aur hafta wasulenge" and thereafter accused Shiva stabbed PW2 with a knife under his chest and thereafter the other associate namely Chikna and accused Sumit caught hold of PW2 and accused Rahish stabbed PW2 with a knife on his left thigh. PW2 reached his home in injured condition and his brother Ankit took him to LBS Hospital. PW2 further deposed that all the accused persons had attacked him with an intention to kill him. 17) In his cross-examination by accused Rahish, PW2 deposed that he knew accused Rahish for the past five years as he was residing in Block No. 1, Trilokpuri, alongwith Indian Kanoon - 6 his family for last many years. Prior to the incident of 02.07.2016, there was no quarrel between him and accused Rahish. He stated that he raised an alarm at the time of incident but no one came to help as accused persons had a criminal background and the public feared them. Pubic persons had seen the incident but they went away without helping him. The place of incident was a thoroughfare. The site plan was prepared at his instance. IO had inquired about the incident from 2-3 public persons of the locality but PW2 could not tell their names. Other police officials were also present with the IO at that time at the spot but he could not tell Sessions Case No. Page ASJ-04/East/KKD/Delhi how many police officials were present. He denied the suggestion that the accused persons did not stab him or that prior to the incident, PW2 had quarrelled with the family of accused Rahish and due to enmity, the accused persons had been falsely implicated by PW2 in the present case. He also denied that one Chand, Lali and Tedha were his friends and they had quarrelled with accused Rahish and that the accused persons have been falsely implicated due to the said enmity. 18) In his cross-examination by accused Shiva, PW2 deposed that the distance between his house and the place of incident was about 100 steps. It took him 2-3 seconds to reach home from the spot. He reached hospital within five minutes from his house and the police officials met him at the hospital within 5-10 minutes of reaching there. His statement was recorded in the hospital and he had signed two documents at the hospital. He was discharged from the hospital on the next morning. He also deposed that he used to not consume liquor. Sometimes, he used to consume beer. On the date of incident, he was not under the influence of liquor. He did not remember the date when the site plan was prepared and volunteered that it was prepared one day after the incident. At that time, one police official was present with the IO. He knew accused Shiva for the past 15 years and earlier Shiva used to reside on rent at their house. There was no quarrel between him and accused Shiva prior to the incident. He denied that no incident as alleged had occurred. He denied that the accused persons had been falsely implicated in the present case at the asking of some other Sessions Case No. Page ASJ-04/East/KKD/Delhi persons. PW2 denied that he was under the influence of alcohol at the time of incident. 19) In his cross-examination by accused Sumit, PW2 deposed that he had not given the names of the assailants to the Doctor. He was taken to the hospital on the bike of their neighbour, whose name he did not know. PW2 had gone up the stairs of his house and covered the distance from the spot to his house on foot. Blood had fallen on the stairs, on the clothes of his brother Ankit, on the motorcycle as well as on the road near his house. The IO did not collect the clothes of his brother nor were the same handed over to the police by his brother. He denied that he was under the influence of liquor when he was taken to the hospital. He denied that being under the influence of liquor, he fell on a pointed iron railing and sustained injuries on his person. He denied that the incident as alleged did not take place. PW2 deposed that the accused had made disclosure statement in his presence. He denied that accused did not make any disclosure statement in his presence or that he did not visit the police station on 04.07.2016. He denied that he had signed the arrest documents later at the instance of IO in the police station and for that reason he did not sign the disclosure statement of the accused on 04.07.2016. PW2 denied that accused Sumit was at his house with his family at Indian Kanoon - 7 Vasundhara Enclave at the time of incident. He denied that he had falsely implicated the accused persons due to previous grudge and enmity. 20) Bhag Chand, father of injured Ravi was examined as PW3 and he deposed that on 03.07.2016, he handed over one Sessions Case No. Page ASJ-04/East/KKD/Delhi black colour T-shirt which was worn by his son Ravi at the time of incident to ASI Shiv Murti, who had come to their house at night. The said T-shirt had two cuts and the same was seized by the IO vide seizure memo Ex. PW3/A. He identified the T-shirt as Ex. P2. 21) Ankit, brother of injured Ravi was examined as PW5 and he deposed that on 02.07.2016 at about 10:00-10:15 p.m., he was present at his house when his brother Ravi came home in injured condition. Ravi was bleeding at that time and he had stab injury on his chest and back. Ravi told him that Rahish and Shiva had attacked him with a knife alongwith their associates. PW5 took Ravi to LBS Hospital where he was medically treated. 22) In his cross-examination, PW5 deposed that he had not seen the incident. The accused persons were known to his brother Ravi as they were living in the same neighbourhood. Accused Sumit was also known to Ravi. He affirmed that he had given the names of the accused persons as told to him by his brother Ravi. He also deposed that Ravi had informed him about the name of accused Sumit, who was also present with other accused persons. He stated that he had told the name of Sumit to the police in his statement recorded under Section 161 Cr.P.C. and he denied the suggestion to the contrary. He stated that he had made a call at 100 number regarding the incident and denied the suggestion that he had not stated about the same in his statement recorded under Section 161 Cr.P.C. as he had not made any call at 100 number. He also denied that his brother Ravi was under the influence of liquor and being in drunken condition he Sessions Case No. Page ASJ-04/East/KKD/Delhi fell on pointed iron railings and sustained injuries on his own. He also denied that before shifting his brother Ravi to the hospital, he did not meet his brother Ravi or that Ravi did not tell him anything against the accused persons. He denied that due to previous enmity between his brother and accused persons, he had falsely deposed against the accused persons. 23) Injured Ravi was medically examined at LBS Hospital at 10:40 p.m., vide MLC No. 9486 of 2016 (Ex. C1). As per the MLC, he was brought by his brother Ankit. The MLC reveals the history of assault. Injured Ravi was found positive for the smell of alcohol. On his examination following injuries were found present:- (i) sharp incised wound of size 2.5 cm x 1 cm x 1.5 cm over lower left side chest; and (ii) sharp incised wound 4 cm x 1.5 cm over left thigh outer part lower one third. The nature of injury was opined to be "simple" in nature vide Ex. C2. 24) The Doctor had also handed over the clothes of injured Ravi i.e. one black colour baniyan (vest) and one black colour pant (lower), to the IO which were seized vide seizure memo Ex. PW4/E. Indian Kanoon - 8 25) During investigation, the exhibits were sent to FSL for analysis. As per the FSL result Ex. C3, blood was detected on exhibit 1 (one pair of chappal); exhibit 2 (gauze cloth piece of injured Ravi); exhibit 3 (concrete material having blood stained Sessions Case No. Page ASJ-04/East/KKD/Delhi earth); exhibit 6 (one black lower of injured Ravi); exhibit P7 (one black cut/torn sleeveless baniyan of injured Ravi) and exhibit P8 (wet gauze cloth with blood sample of injured Ravi). 26) Blood was not detected on exhibit 4 (concrete material described as earth concrete); exhibit 5 (one cut/torn black T-shirt of injured Ravi). 27) As per the FSL result Ex. C3, the blood found on exhibit 1 (pair of chappal); exhibit 2 (blood stained gauze); exhibit 6 (lower); exhibit P7 (baniyan) and exhibit P8 (blood stained gauze) was of 'B' Group. Ex. P8 was the blood sample of victim Ravi which was of 'B' blood group which was also detected on the pair of chappals seized from the spot, the lower and the vest of injured Ravi which were handed over by the Doctor to the IO at the time of the medical examination of injured Ravi vide MLC Ex. C1. 28) With regard to evidentiary value to be attached to testimony of an injured witness, it would be worthwhile to consider the principles of law laid down by Hon'ble Supreme Court in this regard. 29) As regards the testimony of an injured witness, in Abdul Sayeed v. State of M.P., (2010) 10 SCC 259 the Hon'ble Supreme court made the following observations: "Injured witness The question of the weight to be attached to the evidence of a witness that was himself injured in the course of the occurrence has been extensively discussed by this Court. Where a witness to the occurrence has himself been injured in the incident, Sessions Case No. Page ASJ-04/East/KKD/Delhi the testimony of such a witness is generally considered to be very reliable, as he is a witness that comes with a built-in guarantee of his presence at the scene of the crime and is unlikely to spare his actual assailant(s) in order to falsely implicate someone. "Convincing evidence is required to discredit an injured witness." 30) In Abdul Sayeed v. State of M.P., (supra), the Hon'ble Supreme Court also reiterated the special evidentiary status accorded to the testimony of an injured accused and relying on its earlier judgments and noted the decision in Shivalingappa Kallayanappa v. State of Karnataka [1994 Supp (3) SCC 235 : 1994 SCC (Cri) 1694] wherein it was held that the deposition of the injured witness should be relied upon unless there are strong grounds for rejection of his evidence on the basis of major contradictions and discrepancies, for the reason that his presence on the scene stands established in case it is proved that he suffered the injury during the said incident. The Hon'ble Supreme Court further observed as follows: Indian Kanoon - 9 "30. The law on the point can be summarised to the effect that the testimony of the injured witness is accorded a special status in law. This is as a consequence of the fact that the injury to the witness is an inbuilt guarantee of his presence at the scene of the crime and because the witness will not want to let his actual assailant go unpunished merely to falsely implicate a third party for the commission of the offence. Thus, the deposition of the injured witness should be relied upon unless there are strong grounds for rejection of his evidence on the basis of major contradictions and discrepancies therein.'' 31) From the testimony of PW2, it emerges that on the date of incident i.e. 07.2016, when Ravi went to take a cold drink from sewing centre near park after having dinner, at about 10:15 p.m., the accused Rahish @ Aadil, Shiva @ Peer (since Sessions Case No. Page ASJ-04/East/KKD/Delhi deceased) and Sumit @ Chatela came there. All had knives in their hands. They abused Ravi, PW2 and when PW2 asked them the reason, they surrounded PW2 and accused Shiva threatened PW2 Ravi that they would kill him and would create terror in the area and extract money from people. Thereafter, accused Shiva stabbed Ravi with a knife under his chest and one Chikna and accused Sumit caught him and accused Rahish stabbed Ravi with a knife on his left thigh. Ravi got seriously injured and rushed to his house. The accused persons ran away from the spot. 32) Accordingly, in light of law discussed hereinabove, it is manifest that the complainant Ravi in his testimony recorded before the Court has detailed the manner in which accused persons had caused injuries to him, after which they ran away from the spot. Despite detailed cross-examination of PW2 Ravi the accused persons were unable to cause any dent in his testimony recorded before the court. PW Ravi remained steadfast and did not detract from his version given in his examination-in-chief. The testimony of PW2 Ravi is also consistent with his statement given to the police Ex. PW2/A. No strong ground has been made out for rejection of his testimony, there being no major contradictions or discrepancies in his testimony. 33) As per the MLC of injured Ravi Ex. C1, two incised wounds were present on his body - one on his lower left side of the chest and another over left thigh. Accordingly, the injuries found on the body of injured Ravi also corroborate the oral testimony of injured Ravi, who deposed that accused Shiva had Sessions Case No. Page ASJ-04/East/KKD/Delhi stabbed him with a knife under his chest and another stab injury was given to him by accused Rahish on his left thigh. 34) The testimony of PW Ravi is also corroborated by the testimony of his brother PW5 Ankit, who deposed that on 02.07.2016 at about 10:00-10:15 p.m., when he was present at his house his brother Ravi came there in injured condition and was bleeding. He had stab injuries and he was told by Ravi that he was attacked by Shiva and Rahish with knives alongwith their associates. He took his brother to the Indian Kanoon - 10 hospital. In his cross- examination, he also revealed the name of accused Sumit regarding whom his brother had informed, who was also present alongwith the other accused persons at the time of incident. 35) In view of the aforesaid discussion it is manifest that the testimony of injured Ravi stands corroborated by testimony of his brother as well as the medical and forensic evidence. The FSL result also corroborates the testimony of injured Ravi regarding injury caused to him at sewing centre near the park and the blood group found on the lower and the vest, which were handed over to the IO by the doctor at the time of medical examination, was found to be the blood group of injured Ravi. As per the testimony of injured Ravi, he sustained two stab injuries at the time of incident which are also reflected in his MLC Ex. C1. 36) It is contended by the Ld. Counsel for the accused persons that perusal of the MLC of injured Ravi reveals that he was found positive for alcohol, whereas he has deposed that he Sessions Case No. Page ASJ-04/East/KKD/Delhi had not consumed liquor on the date of incident thereby pointing to false implication. It is urged that on the date of incident, injured Ravi had fallen on pointed iron railings in drunken condition and he sustained injuries because of the same and no injuries were inflicted upon Ravi by the accused persons. It is also argued that as per the FSL result Ex. C3, blood could not be detected on T-shirt Ex. P2 which was allegedly worn by Ravi at the time of incident. 37) No doubt, injured Ravi was found positive for alcohol at the time of incident, however, merely because he had deposed that he did not consume alcohol on the date of incident would not absolve the accused persons of the offence in issue. Injured Ravi had denied the suggestion that he had fallen on pointed iron railings and sustained injuries while he was under the influence of alcohol at the time of incident. No evidence was led by the accused persons to show that the injured had sustained injuries by falling on some pointed iron railings. On the other hand, the injured Ravi has specifically deposed regarding the manner in which the injuries were caused to him with knives by the accused persons. The MLC of injured Ravi reveals two sharp incised wounds. There is no evidence brought on record to show that the sharp incised wounds could have been caused by any object other than knives as stated by injured Ravi. In these circumstances, the accused persons have failed to prove that injured Ravi sustained injuries by falling on pointed iron railings. The fact that blood was not detected on the T-shirt handed over by PW3 to IO cannot be read in favour of the accused persons as the blood of injured Ravi was detected on the clothes i.e. his vest Sessions Case No. Page ASJ-04/East/KKD/Delhi and lower/pajama handed over by the Doctor to the IO at the time of medical examination of injured Ravi vide his MLC Ex. C1. 38) It is also contended by the ld. counsel for accused persons that though injured Ravi had stated that the disclosure statement of accused persons Shiva and Rahish Indian Kanoon - 11 were recorded in his presence, however, their disclosure statements do not bear their signatures. In this regard, it is noted that the signatures of injured Ravi are present on the arrest memo of accused Rahish and Shiva. However, absence of signatures of injured Ravi on disclosure statement of accused Rahish and Shiva would not be fatal to the prosecution case as his signatures are not required on the disclosure statement of accused persons. 39) In his statement recorded under Section 313 Cr.P.C., accused Rahish stated that complainant had falsely implicated him in the present case due to previous enmity with him and his friends. About one month prior to the present case, the friends of the complainant has caused gun shot injury on his chest and also caused multiple stab injuries on the face and thigh of accused Rahish regarding which an FIR was registered at PS Kalyanpuri. The complainant falsely implicated him in the present case to pressurize him to settle that matter. 40) In this regard, it is noteworthy that accused Rahish has not brought any evidence on record to connect the complainant with the alleged FIR regarding injuries caused to him by the friends of the complainant. No evidence regarding the Sessions Case No. Page ASJ-04/East/KKD/Delhi said incident has been brought on record. The complainant Ravi was not cross-examined on that aspect and no suggestion regarding the said alleged incident have been put to the complainant Ravi or any other prosecution witness. Only bald suggestion has been given that one Chand, Lali and Kheda were friends of Ravi and they had quarrelled with accused Rahish, which suggestion was denied by injured Ravi. No further details regarding previous enmity were put to the complainant. Even though, it is asserted that an FIR had been registered regarding the previous incident, however, the said FIR was also not put to the complainant during his examination nor was the same brought on record. In these circumstances the defence sought to be raised by accused Rahish remains unsubstantiated and not proved. 41) In his statement recorded under Section 313 Cr.PC, accused Sumit @ Chatela stated that the complainant used to create commotion and abuse him from outside his house after consuming liquor. Sumit used to object to his behaviour and as such the complainant used to keep enmity towards him. Even prior to the present incident, the complainant had caused injury on his face with a blade, however, accused did not lodge any complaint against him as the matter was sorted out by the locality persons. Accused still had a cut mark on his face. He never caused any injury to the complainant and rather he himself received injury but he was falsely implicated in the present case. 42) In this regard, it is noteworthy that though accused Sumit had raised the defence that he had objected to the Sessions Case No. Page ASJ-04/East/KKD/Delhi behaviour of the complainant on earlier occasion due to which the complainant had enmity towards him, however, no suggestion in that regard was put to the complainant by accused Sumit at the time of his cross-examination before the court. Accused Sumit has also referred to a prior incident when the complainant had caused injury on his face with a blade, however, no cross- examination of the complainant was conducted on that aspect and only a bald suggestion has been put to the Indian Kanoon - 12 complainant that he had falsely implicated the accused persons due to previous grudge and enmity. The defence sought to be raised at the stage of recording of statement under Section 313 Cr.P.C. has been raised at a belated stage without there being any suggestion or cross-examination of the witnesses on that aspect. Accordingly, accused Sumit has failed to prove the defence sought to be raised by him. 43) Admittedly, the weapon of offence has not been recovered. However, it is settled law that non-recovery of weapon of offence is not fatal to prosecution case. 44) Hence, the examination-in-chief of the complainant PW2 is corroborated by the testimony of his brother PW5. The testimony of injured Ravi is also corroborated by medical evidence on record and his version is also consistent with his initial statement given to the police vide Ex. PW2/A. 45) In this regard it is noteworthy that it is settled law that any deficiencies during investigation shall not accrue to the benefit of the accused. The contradictions and inconsistencies as Sessions Case No. Page ASJ-04/East/KKD/Delhi pointed out by Ld. Counsel for the accused persons are only minor in nature and are not material inconsistencies on the basis of which the prosecution case can be dislodged. 46) The accused persons in the present case have been charged for attempt to murder under section 307 Cr.P.C. read with Section 34 IPC. Section 307 IPC relating to attempt to murder provides as under : "Whoever does any act with such intention or knowledge, and under such circumstances that, if he by that act caused death, he would be guilty of murder, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine, and if hurt is caused to any person by such act, the offender shall be liable either to imprisonment for life, or to such punishment as is hereinbefore mentioned." 47) In order to determine if the accused persons are guilty of the offence under section IPC, it would be worthwhile to consider the decision of Hon'ble Supreme Court of India in the matter of State of Madhya Pradesh v. Kedar Yadav, 2009 (17) SCC 280, wherein the Hon'ble Supreme Court of Indian enunciated the law in relation to Section 307 IPC and observed that for conviction under section 307 IPC it is sufficient if there is an intention or knowledge coupled with some overt act in execution of such intention / knowledge. It is not necessary that bodily injury capable of causing death should have been inflicted. If the act, irrespective of its result is done with the intention or knowledge and under the circumstances mentioned in the section, the same may result conviction under section 307 IPC and the accused cannot be acquitted merely because the Sessions Case No. Page ASJ-04/East/KKD/Delhi injuries inflicted on the victim were in the nature of a simple hurt. 48) Accordingly in order to prove the offence under section 307 IPC it is essential to prove that there was intention or knowledge coupled with some overt act in execution of such intention / knowledge. Indian Kanoon - 13 49) Similarly, in Vikash Bhardwaj @ Sonu and Anr. V. State of NCT of Delhi, 2014 (2) JCC 996 : 2014 (15) R.C.R. (Criminal) 63, the Hon'ble Delhi High Court culled out the ingredients of section 307 IPC and observed as follows: "21. The essential ingredients required for a conviction under section 307 of the IPC are: "(i) That the death of a human being was attempted; (ii) That such death was attempted to be caused by, or in consequence of the act of the accused; (iii) That such act was done with the intention of causing death; or that it was done with the intention of causing such bodily injury as; (a) the accused knew to be likely to cause death; or (b) was sufficient in the ordinary course of nature to cause death, or that the accused attempted to cause death by doing an act know to him to be so imminently dangerous that it must in all probability cause (a) death, or (b) such bodily injury as is likely to cause death, the accused having no excuse for incurring the risk of causing such death or injury. All the ingredients of the offence must be present before a conviction can be ordered. The injuries sustained, the manner of the assault and the weapons used are all relevant factors. The intention or knowledge which is foremost ingredient of section 307 of the IPC must precede the act attributed to the accused. This intention/knowledge has to be gathered from the circumstances and not necessarily from the ensuing result." (emphasis supplied) Sessions Case No. Page ASJ-04/East/KKD/Delhi 50) Viewed in this light, the testimony of the witnesses has to now be examined to consider whether the conviction can be sustained under section 307 IPC on the basis of the evidence led in the present matter. As discussed herein above, before the conviction can be sustained under section 307 IPC, all the ingredients of section 307 IPC ought to be fulfilled. The injuries sustained, the manner of assault and the weapon used are all relevant factors. The foremost ingredients of section 307 IPC are the intention or knowledge which must precede the act attributed to the accused. The intention / knowledge has to be gathered from the surroundings circumstances and not necessarily from the results that follow, which may result in simple or grievous injury. In order to prove an offence under section 307 IPC essential mens-rea to constitute the said offence has to be proved. Mens-rea can be inferred from the kind of weapon used, nature of injury, place of injury, motive etc. The onus lies on the prosecution that the accused has caused an act with intention/knowledge and under such circumstances that if by that act death was caused, he would be guilty of murder. 51) In the present case the accused persons surrounded the victim Ravi when he had gone to the sewing centre to get cold drink at bout 10:15 p.m. At that time the accused persons inflicted knife injuries upon PW2, Ravi. Two incised wounds were caused to PW2 Ravi as revealed in MLC Ex. C1. The said wounds were caused on lower left side of chest and over left thigh. The nature of injuries Indian Kanoon - 14 suffered by PW2 Ravi were opined to be simple in nature. Sessions Case No. Page ASJ-04/East/KKD/Delhi 52) PW2 deposed that the accused persons had knives in their hands at the time of incident and the injuries were caused to him by knives. The alleged weapon of offence was not recovered, however, the fact that it was a sharp weapon is indicated in the fact that the nature of wounds received by PW2 were incised wounds. It is thus proved on record that PW2 Ravi received two incised wounds at the time of incident and the said wounds were inflicted on vital parts of his body i.e. below his chest and on his left thigh. 53) Pertinently, it is also deposed by PW2 Ravi that after surrounding him, accused Shiva had threatened him by saying that they would kill him and create terror in the area and extract money from people. Thereafter, accused Shiva stabbed him with a knife under his chest and thereafter their associate Chikna and accused Sumit caught hold of him and accused Rahish stabbed him with a knife on his left thigh. Accordingly, the intention of the accused persons while inflicting knife injuries upon injured Ravi was to kill him in order to create terror in the area and with that intention they inficted two knife injuries upon complainant Ravi, who was alone at the time of incident. The said injuries were inflicted upon vital parts of his body. The injuries were inflicted intentionally. More than one injury was caused to the complainant. 54) As discussed hereinabove, two incised wounds were inflicted at the time of incident upon PW2 Ravi with a knife below his chest and on his left thigh which are vital parts of the body, thereby indicating that the accused persons in furtherance Sessions Case No. Page ASJ-04/East/KKD/Delhi of their common intention, intended to cause such bodily injury as the accused persons knew to be likely to cause death. 55) Accordingly, the prosecution has been able to bring home the guilt of the accused persons namely Rahish @ Aadil and Sumit @ Chatela under section IPC and both the accused persons are accordingly held guilty and thereby convicted of the offence of attempt to murder punishable under section IPC. order DEEPALI Digitally signed by Announced in the open Court DEEPALI SHARMA SHARMA Date: 2023.07.04 on this 04th day of July, 2023 16:35:00 +0530 (Deepali Sharma) Additional Sessions Judge-04 East District/KKD Courts/Delhi. State_vs_Ravi_Anil_on_28_September_2022: IN THE COURT OF MS DEEPALI SHARMA: ADDITIONAL SESSIONS JUDGE −04: EAST DISTRICT KARKARDOOMA COURTS: DELHI CNR No. DLET01−001124−2014 SC No. FIR No. U/s IPC P.S. Mayur Vihar In the matter of : State versus 1) Ravi @ Anil S/o Ramesh Chand, R/o H.No. , Trilok Puri, Delhi. 2) Bhima @ Manoj, S/o Om Prakash, R/o H.No. , Trilok Puri, Delhi. 3) Munesh, S/o Gajender Singh, R/o H.No. , Trilok Puri, Delhi. Sessions Case No. Page ASJ-04/East/KKD/Delhi Date of Institution : 12.01.2015 Date of reserving Judgment : 24.09.2022 Date of pronouncement : 28.09.2022 Appearance For the State : Shri Rakesh Mehta, Additional Public Prosecutor. For accused persons : Shri Naveen Singla, Adv., counsel for accused Ravi @ Anil and Munesh. Sh. Bhuvnesh Kumar and Sh. Ajay Chaudhary, Adv., counsel for accused Bhima @ Manoj. Indian Kanoon - 1 JUDGMENT 1) Briefly stated the facts of the present case are that on 03.05.2011 upon receipt of DD No. 77B Ex. PW9/A that someone had been stabbed in a quarrel at , Trilok Puri, and was being taken to the hospital, IO/SI Dharampal alongwith Const. Dinesh reached at the place of incident i.e. Block No. 29, Trilok Puri, Delhi, where they came to know that the injured had already been shifted to LBS Hospital. No witness was found at the spot. IO alongwith Const. Dinesh reached LBS Hospital and collected the MLC No. 6260 of 2011 of injured Suraj and MLC No. 6262 of 2011 of Sunil @ Tulli. 2) IO recorded the statement of injured Sunil Ex. PW4/A wherein he stated that he used to reside at , Trilok Puri, and used to Sessions Case No. Page ASJ-04/East/KKD/Delhi work as a helper on a truck. On that day i.e. 03.05.2011 he alongwith his friend Suraj r/o , Trilok Puri, were consuming liquor at around 09.30 pm at a park at Block No. 29, Trilok Puri,. Meanwhile, Ravi r/o Block No. 29, Trilok Puri, alongwith Bhima and Munesh, who used to reside at Block No. 33, Trilok Puri and whom he knew from before, came to them and they asked them to offer drinks to them. Complainant Sunil and his friend Suraj refused for the same. At that those three persons gave beatings to him and his friend Suraj. Meanwhile, Bhima held complainant Sunil and Munesh held Suraj and Ravi hit the complainant and Suraj with a pointed object. The complainant was hit on his chest and Suraj was hit on his stomach and back. Both complainant and Suraj were bleeding due to the injury. Those three persons fled away from there. Someone called the police at 100 number. On the basis of the statement of the complainant Sunil @ Tulli Ex. PW4/A, FIR bearing No. was registered on 03.05.2011 u/s IPC. 3) IO conducted further investigation. The exhibits of the injured were seized by the IO vide seizure memo Ex.PW5/A. Case property was deposited in the Malkhana. On 15.05.2011 the accused persons were arrested and their personal search was conducted. IO obtained the opinion regarding injuries on the MLCs of the injured Sunil @ Tulli, which was opined to be 'Simple' in nature and the injury on injured Suraj was opined to be 'grievous' in nature. IO added offence u/s 325 IPC. On 20.09.2011 further investigation was assigned to ASI Birpal Sessions Case No. Page ASJ-04/East/KKD/Delhi Singh, who prepared the draft charge sheet and on 02.02.2012 he handed over the case file to MHC(R) due to his transfer. Thereafter the investigation was assigned to SI Bhanu Kanwaria, who added section 307 IPC in the present case and filed the charge sheet. 4) After completion of necessary investigation charge sheet was filed u/s /34 IPC against the accused persons. On the basis of charge−sheet and the documents submitted with it, the learned Metropolitan Magistrate (East), Karkardooma Courts took cognizance of offences under Section /34 IPC and after complying with the provisions contained in Section 207 Cr.P.C, vide order dated 05.01.2015 committed the case to the Court of Session for 12.01.2015. Charge : 5) Vide order dated 11.02.2015 charge was framed against accused Ravi @ Anil, Bhima @ Manoj and Munesh u/s IPC. Indian Kanoon - 2 Prosecution Evidence : 6) In order to prove its case, the prosecution examined following witnesses. (i) PW1 Dr. Sushil Kumar - he prepared the MLC of injured Sunil vide MLC 6262 of 2011 Ex. PW1/A. Sessions Case No. Page ASJ-04/East/KKD/Delhi (ii) PW2 Dr. Waseem Ahmad, Sr. Surgery - he opined injured Suraj to be not fit for statement vide his endorsement on MLC No. 6260 of 2011 Ex. PW2/A. He also examined injured Sunil, who had been referred to Sr. Surgery and prepared a continuation sheet regarding examination of injured Sunil vide Ex. PW2/B. He also opined the nature of injuries sustained by injured Sunil as ' simple' vide his endorsement on MLC Ex. PW1/A. (iii) PW3 injured Suraj. (iv) PW4 injured Sunil @ Tulli - complainant. (v) PW5 Const. Dinesh − part of investigation, who reached the spot alongwith IO/SI Dharampal and also went with him to LBS Hospital. He took the rukka to the police station for registration of the FIR. After registration of FIR, he handed over the rukka to IO/SI Dharampal at the spot. He went to LBS Hospital and took one sealed parcel and handed it over to IO/SI Dharampal, who seized the same vide seizure memo Ex. PW5/A vi) PW6 ASI Veerpal Singh - Second IO of the case. vii) PW7 Manoj Kumar - brother of injured Suraj. Sessions Case No. Page ASJ-04/East/KKD/Delhi viii) PW8 SI Bhanu Kanwaria - Third IO of the case, who filed the charge sheet. ix) PW9 W/Const. Shakuntala, who recorded DD No. 77D vide Ex. PW9/A. x) PW10 Dr. Vijay Kumar - who identified the handwriting and signature of Dr. Manjoor Ahmed, who opined the nature of injured on MLC of injured Suraj to be 'grievous'. xi) PW11 ASI Dharmapal - First IO of the case. Indian Kanoon - 3 xii) PW12 Dr. Rakesh Singh - who identified the handwriting and signatures of Dr. Priyanka, who had prepared MLC No. 6260 of 2011 of injured Suraj. 7) During trial, on 12.04.2022, ld. Counsel for the accused persons admitted two documents u/s 294 Cr.P.C. i.e. FIR No. dated. 03.05.2011 of PS Mayur Vihar Ex. C−1 and MLC No. prepared by Dr. Priyanka Ex. PW12/A (also exhibited as Ex. C−2) and as such PWs DO/ASI Vikram Singh and Dr. Priyanaka were dropped from the list of witnesses. Sessions Case No. Page ASJ-04/East/KKD/Delhi Statement of accused persons : 8) After conclusion of prosecution evidence, on 05.07.2022 statement of the accused persons Ravi @ Anil, Bhima @ Manoj and Munesh were recorded under Section 313 Cr.P.C wherein they denied the correctness of all the incriminating circumstances appearing in the evidence against them and stated that they were innocent and were falsely implicated in this case. Accused persons did not prefer to lead evidence in their defence. 9) I have heard the arguments of ld. Counsel for the accused persons and Ld. Addl.P.P. for the State and perused the record. Testimonies of material witnesses : 10) Injured Suraj was examined as PW3 and he deposed that on 03.05.2011 at about 30 pm he alongwith his friend Sunil @ Tulli, who was residing at H.No. , Trilok Puri, Delhi, were sitting in a park of 29 Block, Trilok Puri and both were consuming liquor. At that time, accused Ravi, Bhima and Munesh came to them and told them "Hame Bhi Sharab Pillao". Sessions Case No. Page ASJ-04/East/KKD/Delhi 55) Similarly, in Vikash Bhardwaj @ Sonu and Anr. V. State of NCT of Delhi, 2014 (2) JCC 996 : 2014 (15) R.C.R. (Criminal) 63, the Hon'ble Delhi High Court culled out the ingredients of section 307 IPC and observed as follows: "21. The essential ingredients required for a conviction under section 307 of the IPC are: "(i) That the death of a human being was attempted; (ii) That such death was attempted to be caused by, or in consequence of the act of the accused; Indian Kanoon - 14 (iii) That such act was done with the intention of causing death; or that it was done with the intention of causing such bodily injury as; (a) the accused knew to be likely to cause death; or (b) was sufficient in the ordinary course of nature to cause death, or that the accused attempted to cause death by doing an act know to him to be so imminently dangerous that it must in all probability cause (a) death, or (b) such bodily injury as is likely to cause death, the accused having no excuse for incurring the risk of causing such death or injury. All the ingredients of the offence must be present before a conviction can be ordered. The injuries sustained, the manner of the assault and the weapons used are all relevant factors. The intention or knowledge which is foremost ingredient of section 307 of the IPC must precede the act attributed to the accused. This intention/knowledge has to be gathered from the circumstances and not necessarily from the ensuing result." (emphasis supplied) Sessions Case No. Page ASJ-04/East/KKD/Delhi 56) Viewed in this light, the testimony of the witnesses has to now be examined to consider whether the conviction can be sustained under section 307 IPC on the basis of the evidence led in the present matter. As discussed herein above, before the conviction can be sustained under section 307 IPC, all the ingredients of section 307 IPC ought to be fulfilled. The injuries sustained, the manner of assault and the weapon used are all relevant factors. The foremost ingredients of section 307 IPC are the intention or knowledge which must precede the act attributed to the accused. The intention / knowledge has to be gathered from the surroundings circumstances and not necessarily from the results that follow, which may result in simple or grievous injury. In order to prove an offence under section 307 IPC essential mensrea to constitute the said offence has to be proved. Mensrea can be inferred from the kind of weapon used, nature of injury, place of injury, motive etc. The onus lies on the prosecution that the accused has caused an act with intention/knowledge and under such circumstances that if by that act death was caused, he would be guilty of murder. 57) In the present case a quarrel occurred between PW3 Suraj and PW4 Sunil while they were drinking at Block No. 29 Park, Trilok Puri, Delhi,when the accused persons came and told them to offer drinks to them also. PW3 Suraj and PW4 Sunil refused for the same. At that, the accused persons caused injuries to PW3 and PW4. Three incised wounds were caused to PW4 Sunil as a revealed in MLC Ex. PW12/A. Sessions Case No. Page ASJ-04/East/KKD/Delhi The said wounds were caused on upper abdomen, lower chest and lower thorasic region. One incised wound on left side of chest was caused to PW4 Sunil and there was another swelling over face with abrasion and another abrasion was also present over right forearm as a revealed in MLC Ex. PW1/A of injured Sunil. The nature of injuries suffered by PW3 Suraj were opined to be grievous in nature and the nature of injury suffered by PW4 Sunil were opined to be simple in nature. Both PW3 and PW4 deposed that a sharp object was used to cause the injuries. The alleged weapon of offence was not recovered, however, the fact that it was a sharp weapon is indicated in the fact that the nature of wounds received by PW3 and PW4 were incised wounds. It is thus proved on record that PW3 Suraj received three incised wounds and PW4 Sunil received one incised wound. The said wounds were inflicted on vital part of their body i.e. on their chest and abdomen also with a sharp object. Indian Kanoon - 15 58) No reason for false implication of the accused persons has been brought on record and only a bald plea has been taken in statement of the accused recorded under section 313 Cr.P.C. that the accused persons have been falsely implicated in the present case. No defence evidence has been led to substantiate the said plea. Infact PW4 and PW7 deposed that they knew the accused persons beforehand and there was no enmity or quarrel between them and the accused persons. They did not know if there was any enmity / quarrel between PW3 Suraj and accused persons. They affirmed that Suraj used to consume liquor with the Sessions Case No. Page ASJ-04/East/KKD/Delhi accused persons. Hence, no reason to falsely implicate the accused persons has been brought on record. Accordingly, the defence taken by the accused persons in their statements recorded u/s 313 Cr.P.C. that they have been falsely implicated in the present case remains unsubstantiated. 59) As discussed hereinabove, multiple incised wounds were inflicted at the time of incident upon PW3 Suraj and one incised wound was inflicted upon PW4 Sunil, with a sharp object, on their abdomen and chest region, which are vital parts of the body, thereby indicating that the accused persons in furtherance of their common intention, intended to cause such bodily injury as the accused persons knew to be likely to cause death. 60) Accordingly, the prosecution has been able to bring home the guilt of the accused persons under section IPC and the accused persons are accordingly held guilty and thereby convicted of the offence of attempt to murder punishable under section IPC. Announced in the open Court on this 28th day of September, 2022 (Deepali Sharma) Additional Sessions Judge−04 East District/KKD Courts/Delhi. Sessions Case No. Page ASJ-04/East/KKD/Delhi Indian Kanoon - 16
